author: @gregcooper8407
text: You shouldn’t need the pulley weld a weight to the hub side of the drum.  It will already be over the centre line of the axle due to the overlap in the centre.  You can then attach a recoil spring between the hub sides of the drum to pull them back together in low wind speeds.  The spring/weight will act as a governor.  You will need come calculations regarding how much weight to be able to move the drum and a what wind speed it will start to close.  This concept will rely on constant wind speed and will likely not respond well to the sort of gusty wind we have here in the UK

author: @Barskor1

author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
text: A simpler idea would be to have a shaft horizontally that can pivot. The half barrels mount to each end. The in the center attached to your vertical drive shaft is a weight extended upward. If it is off center just a bit and as it spins faster the weight will through out tilting the barrels to catch less wind.

replies.author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
replies.text: may want to orientate the barrels horizontal as well. This way axis of the barrel is then in alignment of the tilting shaft so it will not have resistance biasing from one end of the barrel or the other when it is coming back around against the wind.

author: @JacoKruger.
text: for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction, keep this law in mind, you will find your sliders with the pully will have a problem

author: @jasonwitt8619
text: I know how to make it work mate, Put weights on the inside part of the drums closest to the center and put each half of the drum on tracks so they can slide just as you where showing on the table. The faster the drum spins the weight will try to pull to the center. This is why the earth has all the heavies towards the center on the earth as it spins. The slower the drum spins the wind will catch the edge of the cup of each drum and pull the two halves outward. So all you will have to do is put weights and tracks on the two halves. Problem solve. But this will make them heavier. Because you need to use some heavy weights to make this work correctly. The weights need to be at least 10 times that of the drums and the tracks need to have strong bearing's to withstand the weight.

author: @themeek351

author: @matthewnicholson1560
text: I think you are going to create some very interesting fluid dynamics when the drums are approximately half way across each other and the air escapes between  the wind cupping half to behind the other half. It may be necessary to create a baffle to prevent this flow to avoid catastrophic self destruction when the the flow of air changes direction.

replies.author: @HansFrisk
replies.text: This effect should be built into the oildrum design, starting out they should cover each other slightly since the air escaping to the other drum somewhat destroys the tail-eddy of the drum facing into the wind. roughly 30% overlap is a good startout point. If someone ever mentions "Modern Savonius blade design" it's this feature of torque transfer from the wind catching blade to the wind breaking one.

author: @BiosWars
text: As the Savonius Wind Turbine begins to close. The force of the wind will effect the centers ability to spin. It's a great concept, but you'll have to consider an expandable, retractable center shield or wall that will help stop the drag, or canceling out of wind force.

author: @dimensionprint5096
text:
Cool idea, but wouldn't it make more sense to use a generator with multiple phases which get switched on/off based on speed? Each added phase would act as an additional brake to turbine and at the same time produce more electricity thus harnessing more power from the wind. Less mechanics, easier  to make, cheaper, increases reliability and increases electricity production.

The electronics could be as simple as a 12v relay. The generator will reach 12v generation at a certain minimum speed which then activates the 2nd generator phase and so on.

author: @mrdewilliams
text:
That is quite brilliant!

Some years back, I tried my hand at making wind driven wooden automata.  They lasted less than a week before wind speeds send pieces splintering in every direction.  Ultimately, I was wanting to make a large scale automata (with aeolian harp like elements), so I came up with the idea of driving it using a squirrel cage, but place it inside a louvered box.  It was then my plan to open and close the louvers with something along the lines of a Watt Governor.  One thing after another led to the whole project being back-burnered.

It never occurred to me that instead of controlling the wind by a housing for my squirrel cage, I could try opening and closing the blades of the cage itself.

I am looking greatly forward to seeing how Luke's system pans out.

author: @scotttovey
text:
You'll need a recoil spring to open it back up when the wind dies down.
To get it right, the recoil spring should be slightly stronger than your weight.
On the other hand, if you don't close it all the way, the wind might be able to open it back up.
This will be a good mechanical engineering skill builder.

replies.author: @OverlandOne
replies.text: Good points.  Also, as I posted above, Both sections need to move toward the center of rotation together and not just one drum half as Bob mentioned.  If not, the entire device will be way off the center of rotation and extremely out of balance while it is slowing down and still turning at a lower rate.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@OverlandOne 
Yes, that would take a pulley with two lines that affix to the far ends of the barrel halves.
The pulley would then turn as the weight moves outward drawing the two barrel halves together at the same time and keeping them balanced.
You could also use a large pulley that turns around the main shaft which has two slots 120 degrees apart. Each slot would have a rod inserted in them from each barrel half. As the weight moves away from center, the pulley would turn and the rods would rid along the slot pulling the barrel halves together.

author: @cyril3935
text:
Dear Robert,

Hi from France, !
I have followed recently your videos and I will subscribe to your channel quickly.
One of the best way of improvement could be to "load" the spinning rotor with a fluid and dense material, depending of the wind's speed. One which is easy but demanding is ...a spinning water ballast. You could be able to obtain a high torque at high winds, and to be able to maintain an ability to produce at low speed by emptying the ballasts.

One another major improvement could be to use "an English wheel" in order to obtain a curvature on the bottom side, the one which is bolted onto the flat rotating panel.
This will allow a considerable stiffing technique, of course its add a cost by adding a construction time and a specific tool.

But it will be a great way to use classic rivets, able to withstand mechanical failures by fatigue. It is very important to reduce the wobbling of the upper side of the 3 petals. With the time you could obtain some cracks located onto the reduced area of joining.

I have lots of ideas to submit, it will be a pleasure to share them with you. Here is my e-mail : r o u l e p o u p o u l e (a t) r  i  s  e  u  p . n  e  t
Best regards.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like the water ballast idea mate

author: @stuartevans6807
text: How about adding weight to the central edge of the drum, enough weight to make the centre of gravity of the half drum to be on the opposite side of the axle. As it spins centrifugal forces will close it automatically. A spring/weight to pull it back out again.

replies.author: @timothygorman7528
replies.text: I was thinking the same thing, but you said it better than I ever could have!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice!!!

replies.author: @stuartevans6807
replies.text: Or even a boom with a weight on the end so you wouldnt need as much weight

replies.author: @timothygorman7528
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering So, I can't imagine a wind so strong you would need the "clam shells" to completely close.  I don't know the proper calculations, but a spring mounted between the inner halves and calculated weight mounted to those inner halves could do the trick.  As it spins faster, the weight wants to throw out from those two central halves, essentially closing the "clam shell,"  while being restricted by expanding the spring.  When the wind dies down, the spring retracts, overcoming the centrifugal force of the weights as it slows down, reopening the clam shell.  The trick is the spring - for example, a "Trampoline" spring would be too strong to overcome except at extremely high speeds - however, with the weight of the barrel halves, you would need quite a strong spring to retract the halves.  Calculated correctly though, it could act as a spring/counterweighted governor in a sense, couldn't it?

replies.author: @Arachnoid_of_the_underverse
replies.text: How about weights on the end of an A frame with a pivot at the top and the centre spar of the frame able to extend on a slider. As the weights are forced outwards, the top peak could be used to pull the section inwards. A spring could be mounted on across the legs of the A frame to pull it back in when not under load.

author: @KAIOSDEFECT
text: Hey Robert, I was wondering how you might design a room which could save internal electronics from a Carrington Event?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sorry mate - never even thought about it

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
All you need to do is put a Faraday cage around the room. 
Or if you really want to protect your equipment, around your house.

There is a paint that has iron particles in it that enable you to paint a wall that will then hold magnets. You have to keep stirring the paint to keep the iron particles floating so that they spread evenly.

Another way would be to paint the walls with Roberts graphene paint. Luke used it to protect an electric guitar's pickups from ambient noise and make it sound better.

Just keep in mind that when you put that Faraday cage around the room, you're not likely to get any cell phone signals inside that room unless you run a repeater antennae cable through the wall.

replies.author: @KAIOSDEFECT
replies.text: ​@scotttovey ​ @Robert Murray-Smith  I'm wondering if an underground room made out of some kind of concrete mix made up with the conductive ink from the shop could work. Or just high density of graphene mixed into concrete. I wonder if a mix like that could handle the current from the sun in the scenario of Earths magnetic field not protecting us. I mean handle solar orbiter style flux of about 17 500 watts per square meter, or near that ball park. SuspicousObservers youtube has me freaked out haha

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: It wouldn't matter as you wouldn't have electricity to use them after anyways. With that said, I do have a loaded, little MP3 and a wind up charger that are protected, so should something like this happen, I'll still have tunes for awhile.  Alternating wraps of a electrical conductor and an insulator is what can protect them.

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Telma electronic retarder? If it can slow a bus.......

replies.author: @401ksolar
replies.text: Since the 1970s I have been trying all kinds of different designs for wind power, I settled on the savonius rotor style with a flywheel in the center, it never over revs , over several iterations of prototypes trying different kinds of mechanical breaking mechanisms I found those to be unsafe or unreliable, the solid welded rotor with heavy bearings running a chain to the generator ran since 2004, it's getting new bearings just this year.

replies.author: @HansFrisk
replies.text: Inherent to the design is that it is always slower then the windspeed, due to the blade "fighting" the wind heading into it.

author: @alexandrusumanariu4118
text: What about the axle? You will need aditional mechanisms to keep it centered leading to additional weight. It is still an interesting ideea.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there are certainly issues but an interesting investigation non the less

author: @bestowicprimer8835
text: A reverse coil?

Wanted to play with a turbine compression experiments with that.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Rather than closing fully in high winds, close to a max of 90% so you still have a little surface area to get decent generation in high winds. This way your stabilising the output regardless of the wind speed.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice one

replies.author: @jeffreyrood8755
replies.text: I would say the same thing. You want it to produce at any speed, just safely to the unit and I'm...those around it.

author: @401ksolar
text: If you beef up the structure holding that together you will find it only spins up too fast if you're blocking the back wash from the returning side and it's native form mine has never overspun it's been an operation since 2004 not sure where you're going with this

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: Or just add a friction brake that engages to some degree in high winds using a spring returned centrifugal weight.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have a lot of complaints for the reverse mate

replies.author: @401ksolar
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering mine has no brakes other than the generator itself so if the batteries are full they will not supply brakes, I actually did some tests years ago, I've never exceeded 320 RPMs on this rotor the fastest wind I've measured has been 80 mph.

author: @edwardcdg
text: Then how does it open back up when the wind abates?  Perhaps a return spring?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: exactly mate

replies.author: @timothygorman7528
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Return Spring?
